does: This paper proposes using distributional reward models p(r|x,y) instead of scalar reward models to address reward hacking in RLHF. The authors show that under both Bayesian inference and KL-distributionally robust optimization frameworks, the KL-regularized RLHF objective yields a closed-form effective reward formula involving the log-sum-exp function. By varying a parameter β, this unified expression recovers and generalizes existing uncertainty-based aggregation heuristics like mean aggregation, worst-case optimization, and uncertainty-weighted optimization as special cases or limits, while the pessimistic branch naturally incorporates reward uncertainty to reduce exploitation of model errors.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental failure mode in RLHF identified by recent research showing that RL algorithms exploit flaws in learned reward models rather than optimizing genuine quality. It builds on prior approaches attempting pessimistic reward modeling and ensemble-based aggregation schemes, which have been explored informally in the community. The paper connects to broader literature on uncertainty quantification in machine learning, distributionally robust optimization techniques, and Bayesian approaches to handling model misspecification. By unifying these disparate heuristics under a single theoretical framework, it responds to the need for principled uncertainty quantification in reward models and clarifies why existing ensemble methods work when they do.
enables: This framework enables more principled design of uncertainty-aware reward models for RLHF by making explicit the assumptions underlying different aggregation choices. It suggests new variations of the effective reward formula through different β values or model assumptions, opening pathways to hybrid pessimism strategies. The theoretical unification facilitates analysis of when each aggregation scheme is appropriate and under what conditions pessimism prevents reward hacking. It could enable development of distributional reward models that directly learn confidence estimates, moving beyond post-hoc ensemble methods. This foundation supports future work on adaptive pessimism, combining model uncertainty with epistemic factors, and scaling these ideas to large language model alignment.
